summary: On my paternal side, the family name was Peters, though it wasn't originally Portuguese. My grandfather, a Portuguese descendant, came from the Azores or Portugal and settled in Oakland. Sadly, he died in a railroad accident when I was very young, so I only remember his shocking white hair. I never knew my paternal grandmother or much about their origins beyond that.

My grandfather came to this country from the Azores as a young man before settling in Oakland. I'm not exactly sure if he was born in the Azores or Portugal, but the Azores was definitely part of his early life. This place is significant because it marks the beginning of my family's journey to America.

Though I don't know precisely where my grandfather was born, it could have been Portugal or the Azores. The family was Portuguese in descent, even though the name Peters is not Portuguese. Portugal represents the ancestral roots of my paternal family, even if the details are a bit unclear.

Oakland was where my grandfather settled when he came from the Azores, and both my parents were born there. After my parents divorced when I was three, I lived in an orphanage in Oakland for about a year and a half before moving in with my aunt and uncle. Later, after my father remarried, we moved back to Oakland to live with him and my stepmother. Oakland is really the central place of my early family life.

My maternal grandfather's name was Heffron, and he came from Missouri. He and my maternal grandmother settled near what is now Petaluma, California, where they farmed. They donated some land to a school, which no longer operates, but it became public land. The Heffrons were not wealthy, but they contributed to the community in their own way.

My maternal grandparents came from Missouri before moving to California. They settled near Petaluma and took up farming there. Missouri is where that side of my family originated before they made the move west.

My maternal grandparents settled around the area now known as Petaluma, California, where they farmed. They donated some land to a school in that area, which later became part of the public domain. Petaluma represents the place where my mother's family established themselves in California.

In the 1940s and 1950s, land in Sacramento and the Bay Area was still quite affordable, around $10 to $20 an acre. My maternal grandparents' land donation was made during this era, showing that it wasn't a wealthy gift but rather a public service. Sacramento represents part of the broader California region connected to my family's history.

My sister Norma still lives in San Francisco, and we remain very close as the only siblings left in the family. San Francisco is where she has made her home, and it represents a connection point for us as adults. Though I grew up mostly in Oakland and other places, San Francisco is important because of my sister.

My mother died young in the county hospital in Alameda due to alcoholism. Alameda County is where some of the hardest moments of my early life took place, including the loss of my mother. It’s a place tied to both family tragedy and the challenges we faced growing up.

After my parents divorced, my father took my sister and me to Buffalo, New York, where we stayed for a time with relatives or someone on his side of the family. I remember the cold and sleeping in a loft with other children, and I even contracted scarlet fever there. Buffalo was a strange but memorable part of my childhood during a difficult period.

I was baptized a Catholic, but my father drifted away from the Catholic Church after the divorce and remarriage. My stepmother was Italian but not active in the Catholic Church, which may have influenced this shift. The Church was part of my early life but became less central as family troubles grew.

After the divorce, my father became very active in the Masonic Order, spending countless hours there while still working as a laundry truck driver. This involvement seemed to take much of his time away from home, leaving my sister and me with our stepmother. The Masonic Order was a significant part of his life during those years.
